metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile `scc_loc.analyze`'s signature with the `Analyzer` protocol defined in parent 3b30: either (a) add a `languages` parameter (even if unused by scc) so the signature matches the protocol, or (b) explicitly document in parent 3b30 that concrete analyzers may have tool-specific signatures and are not called via the protocol directly — but do NOT silently diverge.
evidence: Leaf plan: 'ENTRY POINT: scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable'

Parent 3b30 AC: 'Analyzer protocol + AnalyzerResult (fields loc/complexity/duplication, each optional=None when unsupported)' — the protocol's analyze method is implied by the interface ticket to take (repo_root, languages) per the epic's 'analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
Parent epic 839b: 'Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
Leaf silently drops the `languages` parameter and introduces `scan_roots` — a parameter the parent never names as part of the protocol signature
findings.finding: The leaf defines `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc)` but the parent 3b30 defines the `Analyzer` protocol with signature `analyze(repo_root, languages)`. The leaf substitutes `scan_roots` for `languages`, meaning `scc_loc` does not conform to the parent-defined protocol. This is a redefinition of a deliverable the parent (3b30) specifies differently — a G7 containment violation.
findings.location: Concrete contracts section — ENTRY POINT definition; parent ticket 3b30 AC: 'Analyzer protocol + AnalyzerResult'
scenarios: If the metrics engine calls `analyzer.analyze(repo_root, languages=['python'])` (per the protocol), scc_loc would receive an unexpected keyword argument and raise TypeError.
If scc_loc is registered in the ANALYZERS map and resolved via resolve_analyzer(), a caller following 3b30's protocol contract cannot call it correctly.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `languages: list[str] | None = None` to the signature (scc ignores it since it scans all files, but it keeps the adapter protocol-conformant): `def analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, languages=None, *, run=_run_scc) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable`. Alternatively, if scan_roots is an implementation detail read from config rather than passed by callers, remove it from the public signature entirely and have the function read it from the injected config.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `scan_roots` parameter: either specify how multiple roots are dispatched to scc (e.g., one subprocess call per root, results merged), or remove the parameter and document that only `repo_root` is supported in this ticket, deferring multi-root support explicitly.
evidence: Plan states: 'ENTRY POINT: scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc)'
Plan also states default shells: '["scc","--format","json", <root>]' — only one root argument
engine_b_backends.py:376 confirms scc is invoked with a single directory: harness.run_tool([binary, '--format', 'json', str(repo_root)], ...)
scc CLI accepts one directory per invocation; multiple roots require multiple calls — unspecified in the plan
Evidence class: Partially-verified (scc one-root-per-call is the observed usage pattern; multi-root behavior of the new param is Unverified)
findings.finding: The plan declares `scan_roots=None` as a parameter of `analyze()` but the default runner always invokes scc with a single `<root>` argument, and scc accepts one directory per call. The plan gives no specification of how a non-None `scan_roots` list would be handled (multiple subprocess calls? multiple path args? silently ignored?), leaving a named parameter with an unspecified — and potentially broken — multi-root code path.
findings.location: Concrete contracts — ENTRY POINT: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc)`
scenarios: A caller passes scan_roots=['src/', 'lib/'] — the implementation has no specified behavior, likely silently ignores scan_roots or errors at runtime.
A future consumer relies on scan_roots being honored, gets incomplete LOC data with no error signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) remove `scan_roots` from the signature (simplest; YAGNI-consistent with the rest of the plan) and note it as a future extension, or (b) specify that when `scan_roots` is non-None, scc is invoked once per root and results are merged into a single `files` dict, with max_loc computed over the union.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove `scan_roots` from the entry-point signature (if unused in this ticket's scope) or add a concrete contract specifying how non-None scan_roots changes the scc invocation and add a corresponding AC test.
evidence: The signature declares `scan_roots=None` but the entire contracts section only describes behavior for a single root: 'shells ["scc","--format","json", <root>]'.
No AC test covers a non-None scan_roots invocation.
ABSENCE: no plan section defines what scan_roots controls or how it changes the scc invocation.
findings.finding: The `scan_roots=None` parameter is declared in the entry-point signature but has no specified behavior anywhere in the plan. The concrete contracts section only describes running scc against a single `<root>`; what the implementation should do when `scan_roots` is a non-None list (merge results from multiple runs? run scc once with multiple paths? ignore it?) is left undefined, leaving implementers without a contract and tests without coverage.
findings.location: Concrete contracts — ENTRY POINT signature: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc)`
scenarios: A caller passes scan_roots=['src/'] expecting scoped analysis; the implementer has no spec to follow and may silently ignore the argument, producing full-repo LOC counts instead.
Two implementers independently interpret scan_roots differently (multiple scc invocations vs. multiple path args to one scc call), making the interface non-deterministic across implementations.
findings.suggested_fix: If scan_roots is a future hook for targeted sub-tree scanning, document it as 'reserved / currently unused; must be None' and add an assertion or NotImplementedError guard. If it is intended for this ticket, specify: 'when scan_roots is a non-empty list, scc is invoked once per root and results are merged (later paths override earlier on key collision, or union); when None, scc is invoked with repo_root.'

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default behavior for `scan_roots` in the ENTRY POINT contract (e.g. 'when non-None, scc is invoked once per root and results are merged; when None, repo_root is used as the sole root').
evidence: "ENTRY POINT: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable`"
No subsequent bullet explains what happens when scan_roots is not None
The default invocation shells `["scc","--format","json", <root>]` but does not clarify what <root> becomes when scan_roots is provided
findings.finding: The `scan_roots` parameter is declared in the entry point signature (`scan_roots=None`) but its behavior when non-None is never described — an executing agent cannot know whether to pass each root to scc separately, concatenate them, or ignore them in favor of repo_root.
scenarios: Agent implements scan_roots incorrectly (e.g. ignores it, passes all roots as one argument, or errors) because the contract is silent
No AC exercises a non-None scan_roots path, so the defect would not be caught by tests
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ENTRY POINT bullet: 'When `scan_roots` is non-None and non-empty, invoke scc once per root and merge the resulting `files` dicts (last-write-wins on collision); `max_loc` is recomputed over the merged dict. When None or empty, use `repo_root` as the sole invocation target.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify `scan_roots` semantics in Concrete Contracts (e.g. 'when non-None, each root is passed as a separate scc target; when None, repo_root is used') and add an AC covering the multi-root path, or explicitly document it as a reserved/no-op parameter so implementers and reviewers know to leave it unused.
evidence: 'ENTRY POINT: scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable'
Default run command shown as '["scc","--format","json", <root>]' (singular root, no mention of scan_roots)
No AC exercises a non-None scan_roots value
findings.finding: The entry-point signature declares `scan_roots=None` as a parameter, but neither the Concrete Contracts section nor any Acceptance Criterion describes what this parameter does, what its non-None behaviour is, or how it affects the scc invocation — leaving the parameter semantically unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Concrete contracts section — ENTRY POINT line; Acceptance criteria section
scenarios: Implementer must guess whether scan_roots replaces repo_root, appends additional paths to the scc command, or is reserved for future use — any guess is defensible from the plan text alone.
A downstream consumer passes a non-empty scan_roots and gets silent repo_root-only behaviour, or a crash, with no AC to catch it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `timeout` parameter (e.g., `timeout=60`) to the `subprocess.run` call in `_run_scc`, and catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` in the fail-closed error handling, returning `Unavailable` with an appropriate reason string.
evidence: "default shells ['scc','--format','json', <root>] via subprocess.run"
ABSENCE: no timeout= argument mentioned anywhere in the plan
The fail-closed spec covers FileNotFoundError, non-zero exit, empty stdout, unparseable JSON — but not a hung process
findings.finding: The plan specifies `subprocess.run` to invoke scc but does not specify a `timeout` parameter. If scc hangs (e.g., on a pathological repo, NFS mount, or filesystem stall), the call will block indefinitely, potentially hanging the metrics pipeline with no recovery path.
scenarios: scc is scanning a very large monorepo or a slow/network-mounted filesystem and takes minutes instead of seconds, blocking the entire metrics collection run.
A zombie scc process accumulates across repeated invocations with no timeout guard.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_run_scc`: `subprocess.run([...], timeout=60, ...)` and add `except subprocess.TimeoutExpired as e: raise` or catch it alongside `FileNotFoundError` in `analyze`, logging and returning `Unavailable(f"scc timed out: {e}", _ACCRUING_SINCE)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the timeout/hang-safety trade-off: either add `timeout=` to the `subprocess.run` default runner (e.g. matching harness's 60-second default) or explicitly state that hang risk is acceptable at this call site and why.
evidence: Plan: 'it invokes scc via its own subprocess' / 'run is an injectable runner (default shells ["scc","--format","json", <root>] via subprocess.run)'
engine_b_backends.py:376: `harness.run_tool([binary, '--format', 'json', str(repo_root)], backend=BACKEND_METRIC)` — same scc invocation via the established boundary

harness.py:129-180: run_tool provides timeout (default 60s, env-tunable), POSIX process-group reaping via killpg, FileNotFoundError→'no_tool', OSError→'other' — none of which raw subprocess.run provides by default
Plan rationale 'does NOT import from rebar.grounding' precludes harness.run_tool but does not discuss whether timeout/hang-safety is needed at this call site or how it would be replicated
findings.finding: The plan introduces a raw `subprocess.run` call for scc without acknowledging that `harness.run_tool` — the established subprocess boundary used by every other scc/tool invocation in the codebase — provides timeout enforcement, POSIX process-group reaping (SIGTERM→grace→SIGKILL), and start_new_session isolation that raw `subprocess.run` lacks. The plan's layer-independence rationale is valid, but it does not address what safety properties are traded away or why they are acceptable at this call site.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / ENTRY POINT contract / `_run_scc` default runner
scenarios: scc stalls on a very large repo or a malformed binary — the call blocks indefinitely with no timeout, hanging the metrics computation thread
A grandchild process holds the stdout pipe open — subprocess.run never returns, with no process-group reaping available
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout=60` (or an env-tunable equivalent) to the default `_run_scc` implementation's `subprocess.run` call, and map `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` → `Unavailable` in the fail-closed error handling. This replicates the critical safety property of `harness.run_tool` without importing from grounding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the Rollback section to say 'three new files' (or enumerate them: `analyzers/__init__.py`, `scc_loc.py`, and the test file) so the rollback instruction is unambiguous.
evidence: 'Rollback: revert is a clean delete of the two new files (no existing file is modified).'
Files section lists: src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py, src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/scc_loc.py, tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py — three files
findings.finding: Rollback section states 'revert is a clean delete of the two new files' but the Files Likely Touched section lists three new files (analyzers/__init__.py, analyzers/scc_loc.py, tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py), creating a contradiction about what constitutes a complete rollback.
findings.location: Rollback section vs. Files likely touched section
scenarios: An implementer rolling back the change deletes only two files and leaves behind the orphaned __init__.py package marker, breaking any future attempt to recreate the analyzers package.
findings.suggested_fix: 'Rollback: revert is a clean delete of the three new files (analyzers/__init__.py, analyzers/scc_loc.py, tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py); no existing file is modified.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `git_metrics.py` and `event_metrics.py` actually define `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"` (cite the exact line), or correct the plan to show where this value originates.
evidence: Plan: '`_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"` — the SAME module-level ISO-8601 constant value used by the existing metrics reader modules (git_metrics.py / event_metrics.py both define `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00")'
registry.py:141,149,157: existing REGISTRY entries use `accruing_since="2026-07-18T00:00:00+00:00"` — a different value from the plan's claimed constant
git_metrics.py lines 1-60: no `_ACCRUING_SINCE` symbol visible; search across metrics/ found no match for this constant name
findings.finding: The plan asserts as a concrete contract that `git_metrics.py` and `event_metrics.py` both define `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"`, but this specific value cannot be verified from the plan itself or from visible registry entries (which show `2026-07-18T00:00:00+00:00`). If the actual value in those modules differs, the new module will silently use a wrong timestamp in `Unavailable` responses.
scenarios: If `git_metrics.py` does not define `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"` (or defines a different value), then the 'SAME constant' claim is false and the new module's Unavailable timestamp is unanchored to any existing convention

findings.finding: The plan specifies `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-07-01T00:00:00+00:00"` and claims this constant 'matches the existing reader-module family', but every existing metrics reader module in the codebase uses `"2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"`. The values differ by six months, making the claim factually wrong and potentially producing inconsistent accrual-since semantics across the metrics family.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-07-01T00:00:00+00:00"` to `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"` to match event_metrics, sidecar_metrics, and git_metrics, unless the code_health lens genuinely began accruing on a different date.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the three sub-cases in the AC explicitly (e.g. parametrize `test_bad_output` over: non-zero exit code, empty stdout, and unparseable JSON) so an agent cannot satisfy the criterion with a single-sub-case fixture.
evidence: "non-zero exit AND unparseable/empty stdout each -> Unavailable. PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_bad_output"
'each' implies all three must be verified, but a single non-parametrized test name cannot guarantee this
Taxonomy item 4 (fixture invalidity): the fixture may not actually exercise all claimed sub-cases
findings.finding: The AC `test_bad_output` bundles three distinct failure modes (non-zero exit, unparseable JSON, and empty stdout) into a single test name without specifying that all three sub-cases must be independently covered, risking a fixture that only exercises one path and silently misses the others.
scenarios: Agent writes test_bad_output covering only non-zero exit; unparseable JSON and empty stdout cases are never exercised; tests pass green but two failure modes are untested
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single `test_bad_output` line with: '- [ ] bad output (3 sub-cases, parametrized): (a) run returns non-zero returncode, (b) run returns empty stdout with zero returncode, (c) run returns non-JSON stdout — each -> `Unavailable`. PROVE: `pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_bad_output`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note to `AGENTS.md` (or an ADR) capturing the `analyzers/` layering rule: no import from `rebar.grounding`, and the Rule-of-Three condition under which shared extraction becomes appropriate.
evidence: "analyzers package stays independent of grounding layer (clean layering; avoids a metrics<->grounding dependency)"
"If a third consumer ever appears (Rule-of-Three), extraction to a shared module is deferred to then (YAGNI now)"
"New analyzers live in analyzers/ (NOT the quarantined adapters/)"
ABSENCE: no mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `analyzers/` package with a deliberate layering rule (no import from `rebar.grounding`, Rule-of-Three deferral for shared extraction) that is only documented inline in this plan's prose, with no mention of persisting it to AGENTS.md, an ADR, or a design doc. Future developers adding analyzers will not discover this rule unless they happen to read this ticket.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Files likely touched: src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py
scenarios: A developer adds a second analyzer and unknowingly imports from rebar.grounding, violating the layering rule with no visible record that it was a deliberate constraint.
The Rule-of-Three deferral is forgotten and a second extraction copy is made, leading to the very divergence the rule was meant to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph entry to AGENTS.md under a 'Metrics Analyzers' section stating: (1) new analyzers live in `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/`; (2) they must NOT import from `rebar.grounding`; (3) shared scc-JSON extraction is deferred until a third consumer exists (Rule-of-Three).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
